suggestions.desc: Fine-tune DeepSeek-V3 or Llama-3 using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on the DementiaBank Pitt Corpus. Compare how specialized fine-tuning improves the detection of subtle linguistic markers like 'anomia' (word-finding difficulty) compared to the zero-shot prompting used in the original study.

suggestions.title: Multimodal Acoustic-Text Fusion Model
suggestions.desc: Extend the text-only analysis by extracting acoustic features (pitch, pause duration, speech rate) using Librosa. Build a hybrid classifier that combines LLM transcript analysis with these prosodic features to see if 'how' a patient speaks improves AD detection over 'what' they say.

suggestions.title: Real-Time Cognitive Screening Plugin
suggestions.desc: Build a browser extension or Zoom integration that uses the DeepSeek R1 reasoning model to analyze live speech-to-text streams during telehealth appointments. The tool would provide a 'cognitive health' score to clinicians based on the spontaneity and coherence of the patient's speech.

suggestions.title: Privacy-Preserving Local Inference API
suggestions.desc: Develop a system using llama.cpp or vLLM to run these Alzheimer’s detection models locally on edge devices (like a high-end tablet). This addresses HIPAA privacy concerns by ensuring that sensitive patient transcripts never leave the local device for cloud processing.

title: Context-Aware Misinformation Detection Using Fine-Tuned BERT and BiLSTM with Attention
authors: Gurung, Rakshak, Tkabladze, Nino
abstract: Misinformation spreads fast, and 60% of consumers now question media reliability (Redline Digital, 2023). Manual verification is slow, and most systems still rely on binary real/fake classification, which overlooks nuanced types of misinformation. We propose a multi-class deep learning approach using a fine-tuned BERT model and a custom BiLSTM with attention to better detect categories like satire, conspiracy, and bias. Our models were trained on a balanced subset of the Fake News Corpus using nine distinct misinformation classes. By addressing both class imbalance and linguistic ambiguity, this system enhances contextual understanding and improves detection across varied news content. Our approach demonstrates that scalable, multi-class classification provides a more accurate and insightful solution to misinformation detection.
supervisor: Dr. Md Abdullah Al Hafiz Khan

suggestions.title: Knowledge Graph-Enhanced Fact Verification
suggestions.desc: Integrate a Knowledge Graph (like Wikidata or DBpedia) into the BiLSTM attention layer to verify entity relationships. This allows the model to flag 'conspiracy' content not just by style, but by detecting factual contradictions against a structured knowledge base.

suggestions.title: Stance Detection and Source Bias Overlay
suggestions.desc: Extend the multi-class classifier to perform 'Stance Detection,' identifying if a text is for, against, or neutral toward a specific claim. Build a Chrome extension that highlights biased sentences in real-time as a user reads news articles online.

suggestions.title: Cross-Lingual Misinformation Transfer
suggestions.desc: Apply the fine-tuned BERT model to a non-English subset (e.g., Spanish or Hindi) using mBERT or XLM-RoBERTa. Research whether the linguistic markers of 'satire' and 'bias' identified in the Fake News Corpus generalize across different languages and cultural contexts.

suggestions.title: Automated Newsroom Triage Dashboard
suggestions.desc: Build a real-time monitoring dashboard for journalists that ingests RSS feeds and ranks incoming stories by 'Misinformation Probability.' Use the multi-class output to sort content into folders like 'Needs Verification' or 'Likely Satire' to speed up editorial workflows.

similar: legacy:UR-01|Fall 2019|fake news detection by support vect *

title: Abstractive Summarization of Informal Text: Fine-Tuning Transformers on Reddit Discussions
authors: Ming, Nong, Challa, Arpana, Edward John, Sharon
abstract: In recent years, the rapid growth of social media platforms has led to an information overload, as a result, the ability to compress long and complex texts into short and precise summaries is essential, especially in online discussions and comment sections. Summarizing such content is difficult due to inconsistencies in sentence structure, slang, abbreviations, and the lack of formal grammar. State-of-the-art models such as BART and PEGASUS have shown promising results, but their performance on informal datasets remains lower compared to structured text benchmark. To address these challenges, we fine-tune BART and PEGASUS on the Reddit TIFU dataset, leveraging their transformer-based architectures to improve abstractive summarization of informal text. Our contribution lies in adapting state-of-the-art summarization models specifically for informal, user-generated discussions, making summarization more effective for online platforms. Our fine-tuned model achieves a 6.6% improvement in ROGUEL compared to existing summarization model, demonstrating its effectiveness in generating concise and coherent summaries of Reddit discussions.
supervisor: Dr. Md Abdullah Al Hafiz Khan

suggestions.title: Subreddit-Specific Vocabulary Injection
suggestions.desc: Modify the BART/PEGASUS tokenizer to include domain-specific slang and abbreviations from niche subreddits (e.g., r/WallStreetBets or r/Programming). Evaluate if adding these 'tokens' reduces the hallucination rate when summarizing highly informal technical discussions.

suggestions.title: Community 'TL;DR' Discord Bot
suggestions.desc: Develop a Discord bot that uses the fine-tuned Reddit model to summarize long, multi-user threads into a concise bulleted list. Implement a 'context window' that tracks different user perspectives within the summary to ensure conflicting opinions are represented.

suggestions.title: Hallucination Auditing in Informal Text
suggestions.desc: Create a benchmarking tool to measure 'faithfulness' in abstractive summaries of informal text. Use NLI (Natural Language Inference) to check if the generated Reddit summaries entail the original text, specifically focusing on whether slang is misinterpreted as factual statements.

suggestions.title: Market Sentiment Summarization Engine
suggestions.desc: Apply the summarization model to financial subreddits and combine it with a sentiment analysis layer. The end product would be a dashboard that provides 'Aggregated Sentiment Summaries' for specific tickers, helping investors digest thousands of comments in seconds.

title: Assessing the Performance of Intelligent Agents in Visual Food Recognition Relative to Manual Data Entry
authors: Belfarsi, El Arbi, Ekeocha, Henry
abstract: Accurate dietary assessment remains a critical yet time-consuming task in health and nutrition monitoring. This study benchmarks the macronutrient estimation capabilities of three intelligent vision agents: GPT Vision, Claude, and Gemini against manually logged food data. We unify two distinct datasets: MenuMatch, annotated by a professional nutritionist, and CGMacros, populated through user entries on MyFitnessPal. After flattening and cleaning both datasets, we first assess each model’s performance in calorie estimation. GPT Vision outperforms the others with the lowest percentage error 13.83% and is subsequently used to benchmark the macro estimations of Claude and Gemini. While Claude shows higher carbohydrate and fat estimation errors, Gemini yields the most balanced results across protein 12.55%, carbohydrates 19.57%, and fats 17.07%. These findings reveal strengths and trade-offs in current intelligent agents for visual food recognition, informing the development of more accurate, user-friendly, AI-powered nutrition tracking systems.

suggestions.title: 3D Volumetric Estimation from Dual Photos
suggestions.desc: Extend the 2D vision agents by implementing a system that requires two photos (top and side view) of a meal. Use a geometric algorithm or a specialized vision model to estimate food volume, significantly improving the accuracy of the 'portion size' variable in macronutrient calculation.

suggestions.title: Personalized 'Health-Swap' Recommender
suggestions.desc: Build an application that takes the vision agent’s output (e.g., a burger and fries) and uses a secondary LLM to suggest healthier alternatives from a local restaurant's menu. Use the CGMacros dataset to train the recommender on user preferences and nutritional targets.

suggestions.title: Occlusion and 'Hidden Ingredient' Stress Test
suggestions.desc: Conduct a research study on the failure points of GPT Vision and Claude when food is obscured (e.g., ingredients inside a burrito or soup). Develop a 'probabilistic nutrition' model that estimates a range of macros based on common recipes for identified dishes.

suggestions.title: Restaurant Menu Auto-Calculator API
suggestions.desc: Create a B2B API service for small restaurants to upload photos of their dishes and receive instant, reasonably accurate nutritional labels. Use the MenuMatch expert annotations as a validation set to ensure the API meets industry standards for dietary transparency.

title: Leveraging Data Science for Resilience: Improving Trauma-Informed Care Practice for Adverse Childhood Experience with AI & Data Science Application
authors: Shaik, Mohmmad Arif
abstract: Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) have long-lasting effects on physical health, mental well-being, education, and socioeconomic outcomes. Resilient Georgia (RG), a statewide initiative, seeks to address ACEs through trauma-informed care and data-driven strategies. However, challenges in data collection, analysis, and tracking hinder the effectiveness of these efforts. This study explores the role of data science and interactive visualization tools in improving outcomes for individuals and communities affected by ACEs. A key focus of this research is the development of a data science management application designed to enhance data collection and facilitate real-time decision-making. The application features interactive dashboards that allow stakeholders such as policymakers, healthcare providers, and community leaders to visualize program outcomes and track trends. Users can input data manually, upload files, and generate custom visualizations, making data more accessible and actionable for informed decision-making. By integrating these data-driven tools, RG can improve the monitoring and evaluation of its programs, optimize resource allocation, and ensure that vulnerable populations receive timely support. This study contributes to the growing body of literature on leveraging technology and data science to mitigate the impact of ACEs and promote long-term well-being.

suggestions.title: ACEs 'Service Desert' Mapping Tool
suggestions.desc: Use Geopandas and Plotly to overlay the project's ACE data with geographic locations of trauma-informed care centers. Identify 'service deserts' where high trauma prevalence correlates with low resource availability, providing a tactical map for Resilient Georgia’s funding allocations.

suggestions.title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning
suggestions.desc: Design a federated learning framework that allows different state agencies to train a common trauma-prediction model without sharing raw, sensitive childhood record data. This solves the 'data collection challenge' mentioned in the abstract while maintaining strict HIPAA/FERPA compliance.

suggestions.title: Longitudinal Outcome Prediction Engine
suggestions.desc: Build a predictive model using Scikit-learn to forecast educational and socioeconomic outcomes based on early childhood ACE scores. Use synthetic datasets to simulate how specific trauma-informed interventions (like counseling) might alter the predicted trajectory of an individual.

suggestions.title: Caseworker Decision Support Dashboard
suggestions.desc: Create an interactive React-based dashboard for social workers that visualizes a child's resilience factors and trauma history. Include a 'Risk Flagging' system that uses AI to highlight cases where the combination of ACEs suggests an immediate need for specific interventional resources.

title: Evaluation of hand-crafted features with mask images obtained from PanNuke dataset using Bayesian optimization and machine learning models
authors: Yellu, Siri
abstract: Semantic image segmentation enables computing systems to understand the semantic patterns of image pixels by using deep learning models to classify the pixels into specific labels. The deep-learning models’ performance in image classification has been evaluated by comparing the predicted images using deep-learned features with human-labeled images or mask images. However, there remains a substantial need to investigate the performance of machine learning models that do not use deep learned features but use hand-crafted features. In this project, we perform a comprehensive evaluation of the performance of the eight machine learning models using 46 hand-crafted features extracted from the PanNuke dataset including 5,179 hematoxylin and eosin images with 161,739 cell nuclei, by optimizing feature selection through Bayesian optimization. The evaluation results indicate that the ensemble learning-based models achieve higher performance compared to others across precision, recall, f1-score, and accuracy.

suggestions.title: Hybrid Ensemble: Hand-Crafted + Deep Features
suggestions.desc: Develop a hybrid model that concatenates hand-crafted features (texture, shape, color) with features extracted from a pre-trained ResNet-50. Use Bayesian optimization to determine the optimal weighting for each feature set in the final pixel classification task.

suggestions.title: Feature Importance Interpretability Study
suggestions.desc: Use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) or LIME to analyze which specific hand-crafted features (e.g., Haralick texture or Zernike moments) are most influential in identifying different cell types in the PanNuke dataset. This provides 'white-box' transparency that deep learning models lack.

suggestions.title: Edge-Device Pathology Classifier
suggestions.desc: Optimize the machine learning models (like Random Forest or XGBoost) to run on low-power ARM-based devices (e.g., Raspberry Pi). By avoiding heavy deep-learning dependencies, create a portable, low-cost diagnostic tool for regions without access to high-end GPU servers.

suggestions.title: Automated Bayesian Feature Engineering Pipeline
suggestions.desc: Build a pipeline that automatically generates hundreds of hand-crafted features from the PanNuke masks and uses Bayesian optimization to select the most predictive subset. This reduces the manual labor involved in traditional computer vision feature selection.

title: Leveraging Graph Attention Networks and BERT for Robotic Surgery Report Generation
authors: Buddharaju, Akshay Krishna Varma
abstract: This project focuses on generating surgical reports from robotic surgery videos by leveraging graph-based representations of instrument-tissue interactions. We utilize Graph Attention Networks (GAT) to model these interactions, which are then integrated into a BERT-based language model for caption generation. Our approach enhances the accuracy of automated surgical reporting by capturing spatial and relational dependencies within surgical scenes. The model is evaluated on the Robotic Instrument Segmentation dataset from the 2018 MICCAI Endoscopic Vision Challenge(Endovis-18) and TORS surgery dataset, achieving high performance across multiple metrics, including BLEU-n, Cider, and ROUGE scores. By automating report generation, this study aims to assist healthcare professionals in improving post-surgical care, optimizing procedural efficiency, and enhancing decision-making in robotic-assisted surgeries.

suggestions.title: Temporal-GAT for Procedural Context
suggestions.desc: Extend the Graph Attention Network to include a temporal dimension (LSTM-GAT) that tracks instrument-tissue interactions over time. This allows the model to generate reports that describe 'sequences' of actions (e.g., 'suturing followed by cauterization') rather than just static scenes.

suggestions.title: Zero-Shot Instrument Recognition
suggestions.desc: Research the model’s ability to generate reports for surgeries using new instruments not found in the Robotic Instrument dataset. Use CLIP-based embeddings to see if the GAT can generalize relationships for unseen tools based on their visual similarity to known ones.

suggestions.title: Interactive Surgeon Training Playback
suggestions.desc: Build a web application where surgical residents can upload video clips and receive an automated report. Highlight the specific video frames that triggered certain sentences in the report, helping residents understand the 'key moments' identified by the AI.

suggestions.title: Intraoperative 'Safety Co-Pilot' Alert System
suggestions.desc: Adapt the GAT-BERT model to run in real-time during a simulation. If the model detects a 'high-risk' instrument-tissue interaction (e.g., excessive force or proximity to a critical vessel), it triggers a visual warning, moving from post-hoc reporting to active surgical assistance.

title: Advances in Non-Invasive Glucose Sensing: A Comprehensive In Vitro Analysis
authors: Belfarsi, El Arbi, Flores, Henry
abstract: This study explores non-invasive glucose sensing using infrared (IR) imaging and electrical measurements in an in-vitro setup. Glucose samples (70–200 mg/dL) were prepared by diluting concentrated solutions (700–2000 mg/dL) 1:10 in synthetic blood concentrate, with 2 mg/dL increments. A custom 3D-printed black cuvette holder ensured consistent alignment of components, including either an IR camera or a 1550 nm photodiode, light sources (850 nm LED/laser, 808 nm, 650 nm, or 1600 nm), and a 3 mm skin-mimicking silicone layer. A Region Based Convolutional Neural Network (RCNN) trained on IR images achieved the lowest RMSE of 10.98 mg/dL at 850 nm LED. A Random Forest model using the recorded-to-baseline voltage ratio yielded an R² of 0.786, RMSE of 17.62 mg/dL, and MAE of 14.05 mg/dL. Clarke Error Grid analysis confirmed clinical relevance.

suggestions.title: CNN-LSTM Denoising for IR Signals
suggestions.desc: Implement a deep learning denoising autoencoder to process the raw 1550 nm photodiode signals. Since the 3D-printed setup ensures alignment, use a 1D-CNN to filter out electronic noise and movement artifacts, significantly improving the signal-to-noise ratio for glucose prediction.

suggestions.title: Environmental Compensation Modeling
suggestions.desc: Integrate DHT22 temperature and humidity sensors into the 3D-printed cuvette holder. Use a Random Forest Regressor to model how ambient conditions affect IR absorption in the synthetic blood, creating a more robust calibration curve that works across varied lab environments.

suggestions.title: Smartphone-Based 'Flash' Sensing Prototype
suggestions.desc: Attempt to replicate the in-vitro analysis using a smartphone’s camera and flash (filtered with an IR-pass film). Develop a mobile app that captures the light intensity through a sample and uses the project's established increments (70–200 mg/dL) to estimate glucose levels.

suggestions.title: Wearable 'Form-Factor' Prototype
suggestions.desc: Miniaturize the custom 3D-printed setup into a wrist-based form factor using an ESP32 and a small 1600 nm LED. Build a Bluetooth-connected dashboard that visualizes the 'Glucose Curve' in real-time, moving the project from a stationary bench setup toward a wearable device.

title: Analysis of Climate Change Effects on Bird Migration Patterns Using Long-Term Data
authors: Syed, Aadil, Beyioku, Mary, Aizebeokhai, Osi, Dogbe, Felix
abstract: To investigate how climate change has affected bird migration patterns over the past decades, focusing on changes in migration timing, routes, and population trends. This project will aim to identify correlations between climate variables and observed changes in bird behavior, contributing to conservation efforts and climate change research.

suggestions.title: Predictive Migration Forecasting with LSTMs
suggestions.desc: Use the historical climate and bird data to train a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network that predicts the 'start date' of migration for specific species based on current-year temperature anomalies.

suggestions.title: Multi-Factor Correlation Analysis (NDVI & Wind)
suggestions.desc: Incorporate satellite-derived vegetation indices (NDVI) and wind pattern data from NASA into the dataset to analyze how food availability and flight effort interact with temperature to shift migration routes.

suggestions.title: Dynamic Conservation Buffer Zones
suggestions.desc: Create a GIS-based tool that calculates 'shifting migration corridors' under different IPCC climate scenarios, helping conservationists identify where to buy land for future bird sanctuaries.

suggestions.title: Real-Time Migration Alert System
suggestions.desc: Develop an API-driven dashboard that integrates eBird 'live' sightings with the project’s predictive model to provide real-time alerts to wind farm operators to temporarily halt turbines during peak migration hours.

title: Quantum Machine Learning For Science And Engineering Research
authors: Polisetty, Andrew
abstract: This research project aims to understand and explore the practical applications of Quantum Machine Learning (QML) in solving real-world challenges. By comparing classical machine learning models such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), Neural Networks, Logistic Regression, and Naive Bayes, with their quantum counterparts. Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVM), Quantum Neural Networks (QNN), Quantum Logistic Regression (QLR), Quantum Deep Neural Networks (QDNN), and Hybrid Quantum Models, we gain hands-on experience in advanced machine learning techniques. The project cover diverse domains including cybersecurity, healthcare, industrial engineering, energy management, and supply chain optimization. Each part of project involves working with real-world datasets, preprocessing, parameter tuning (like qubit settings), and performance evaluation using platforms such as PennyLane and Qiskit. Through this project, we not only learn about the theoretical foundations of QML but also develop practical skills in applying quantum models to high-dimensional and complex data for tasks like fraud detection, quality prediction, patient flow analysis, energy efficiency estimation, and predictive maintenance.

suggestions.title: QML Robustness against Barren Plateaus
suggestions.desc: Conduct a research study on the 'Barren Plateau' problem in the Quantum Neural Networks (QNN) used in the project. Implement and test 'Layerwise Learning' or better initialization strategies to see if they improve the training convergence compared to the project's baseline models.

suggestions.title: Quantum-Classical Hybrid for CERN Datasets
suggestions.desc: Apply the project's Hybrid Quantum Models to a specific scientific domain, such as High-Energy Physics (using the HEP-ML dataset). Compare the efficiency of a Hybrid QNN in classifying particle tracks against the classical Neural Network benchmarks established in the original project.

suggestions.title: QML-Enhanced Feature Selection for Drug Discovery
suggestions.desc: Use the Quantum Support Vector Machine (QSVM) to identify key molecular descriptors in chemical datasets for drug discovery. Research whether the quantum kernel's ability to map data into high-dimensional space helps find non-linear patterns that classical SVMs miss.

suggestions.title: Financial Fraud Detection Pipeline
suggestions.desc: Build a pipeline that uses Quantum Logistic Regression (QLR) to detect anomalies in financial transaction data (e.g., the Credit Card Fraud dataset). Evaluate the 'Quantum Speedup' in inference time when running on a simulated QPU compared to a standard Logistic Regression on a CPU.

title: Quantum Anonymous Notification Protocol
authors: Jha, Nitin; Paudel, Prateek
abstract: The scalability of current quantum networks is limited due to noisy quantum components and high implementation costs, thereby limiting the security advantages that quantum networks provide over their classical counterparts. Quantum Augmented Networks (QuANets) address this by integrating quantum components in classical network infrastructure to improve robustness and end-to-end security. To enable such integration, Quantum Anonymous Notification (QAN) is a method to anonymously inform a receiver of an incoming quantum communication. Therefore, several quantum primitives will serve as core tools, namely, quantum voting, quantum anonymous protocols, quantum secret sharing, etc. However, all current quantum protocols can be compromised in the presence of several common channel noises. In this work, we propose an improved quantum anonymous notification (QAN) protocol that utilizes rotation operations on shared GHZ states to produce an anonymous notification in an n-user quantum-augmented network. We study the behavior of this modified QAN protocol under the dephasing noise model and observe stronger resilience to false notifications than earlier QAN approaches. The QAN framework is also proposed to be integrated with a machine-learning classifier, an enhanced quantum-augmented network. Finally, we discuss how this notification layer integrates with QuANets so that receivers can allow switch-bypass handling of quantum payloads, reducing header-based information leakage and vulnerability to targeted interference at compromised switches.

suggestions.title: Noisy Quantum Component Simulation Layer
suggestions.desc: Extend the protocol by implementing a noise injection module using Qiskit or Cirq that simulates decoherence and gate errors. Develop a classical error-correction feedback loop to test the QuANet's robustness against varying hardware fidelity levels.

suggestions.title: Quantum-Classical Hybrid Messaging API
suggestions.desc: Build a Python-based developer SDK that abstracts the QAN protocol, allowing classical applications to send anonymous 'heartbeat' notifications. Market this as a high-security internal alerting system for financial or government infrastructure.

suggestions.title: Entropy Analysis of Quantum Notification Routing
suggestions.desc: Research the information-theoretic anonymity of the protocol by measuring the trace distance between different notification paths. Quantify how many 'noisy' nodes a protocol can tolerate before the sender's identity can be statistically inferred.

suggestions.title: Quantum Whistleblower Gateway for Corporate Intranets
suggestions.desc: Apply the protocol to a practical use case by building a web interface where employees can submit anonymous reports. Use the QAN protocol as the backbone to ensure that even a compromised network admin cannot trace the report back to a specific node.

title: Predicting the Linux Scheduler’s Next Move with Transformers
authors: Naddaf Shargh, Amirmohammad
abstract: We study whether deep learning can help the Linux CFS, which makes fair scheduling decisions without using historical behavior and may preempt tasks that are near completion. We adopt a dataset and baseline LSTM from earlier work and introduce a Transformer model to explore predictive scheduling. We train both on real scheduling traces to learn the next selected task and timing trends, and evaluate them using task classification accuracy and direction accuracy. Our results show that the LSTM remains the stronger baseline and captures CFS patterns more effectively than our Transformer model in this comparison under identical conditions for fairness.

suggestions.title: Hardware-Aware Feature Engineering with eBPF
suggestions.desc: Enhance the dataset by using eBPF probes to capture hardware-level metrics like L3 cache misses and branch mispredictions. Retrain the Transformer model to see if micro-architectural state significantly improves task completion time predictions.

suggestions.title: Real-Time Scheduler Visualization & Debugger
suggestions.desc: Create a desktop dashboard that visualizes the Linux CFS tree alongside the Transformer's predictions in real-time. This tool would allow kernel developers to see exactly where the deep learning model disagrees with the default heuristic.

suggestions.title: Transformer Attention Analysis for Task Grouping
suggestions.desc: Investigate the Transformer’s attention heads to identify which task features (e.g., vruntime vs. nice values) the model prioritizes. Use this research to propose a 'distilled' heuristic that could be implemented in C without the overhead of a full neural network.

suggestions.title: Energy-Efficient Scheduling for ARM-based Mobile Devices
suggestions.desc: Adapt the model to predict not just the next task, but the optimal CPU frequency (DVFS) for that task. Test the model on an Android/ARM environment to evaluate potential battery savings compared to the default governor.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Latency-Aware Priority Boosting in Linux Completely Fair Scheduler
authors: Natter, Joseph
abstract: Tail latency remains a persistent challenge in Linux’s Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS), particularly when short, latency-sensitive jobs compete with longer ones. Traditional boosting heuristics improve tail latency but require manual tuning and generalize poorly across workloads. This project evaluates whether a reinforcement-learning (RL) controller can dynamically apply priority boosts more effectively than fixed heuristics. Using a discrete-event Python simulator modeled after CFS, this project compares baseline CFS, heuristic boosting, and PPO-based learned boosting under mixed workloads. Results show that while heuristics achieve the lowest absolute latency, RL achieves competitive tail-latency reduction with significantly better fairness and adaptability. A state-space study further analyzes how observation richness affects RL performance.

suggestions.title: eBPF-Driven RL Controller Implementation
suggestions.desc: Transition from the Python simulator to a live Linux environment by implementing the RL agent's decisions via eBPF 'sched_ext' (Scheduler Extensions). This allows the RL model to dynamically adjust priorities in a running kernel without a full recompile.

suggestions.title: Multi-Agent RL for Asymmetric Multiprocessing
suggestions.desc: Expand the RL controller to handle heterogeneous cores (e.g., Intel's P-cores and E-cores). Research how the controller can learn to boost latency-sensitive jobs specifically to performance cores while keeping background tasks on efficiency cores.

suggestions.title: Managed Latency-Optimizer for Kubernetes Nodes
suggestions.desc: Develop a sidecar container that monitors pod tail latency and communicates with the host RL-scheduler. Offer this as a solution for high-frequency trading or real-time bidding platforms where microsecond-level tail latency is critical.

suggestions.title: Workload-Specific RL Checkpoints
suggestions.desc: Build a library of 'Pre-trained Priority Weights' optimized for specific applications like database engines (PostgreSQL) or web servers (NGINX). Create a system that automatically switches RL models based on the detected workload signatures.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Traditional Virtual Machines and Micro Virtual Machines
authors: Onisha, Tasnim Akter
abstract: Virtualization technologies form the backbone of modern cloud and serverless platforms, but the balance between performance efficiency and strong isolation remains a key research challenge. Using a comparative literature based methodology, benchmark data from empirical studies are synthesized and normalized across four metrics: startup latency, memory footprint, isolation strength, and scalability. The findings show that traditional VMs such as KVM and Xen provide robust, formally verified isolation but incur higher boot times and memory usage, whereas Micro VMs like Firecracker and Kata Containers achieve lower latency (often < 125 ms) and smaller memory footprints (5–30 MB) while preserving VM-level isolation. Overall, Micro VMs deliver near container responsiveness with VM-grade security, making them suited for serverless and edge environments. As a future direction, this study highlights the need for scalable, formally verified Micro VM architectures for next-generation cloud systems.

suggestions.title: Automated Orchestration for VM-MicroVM Hybrids
suggestions.desc: Develop a middleware that dynamically chooses between a full KVM instance and a Firecracker MicroVM based on the incoming request’s security requirements and expected execution time. Use a decision-tree approach to optimize for both startup speed and isolation strength.

suggestions.title: Serverless 'Cold-Start' Benchmarking Suite
suggestions.desc: Build a commercial-grade benchmarking tool that allows cloud providers to stress-test their MicroVM infrastructure. Include automated reports on 'Memory Overhead per Million Invocations' to help providers calculate the cost-benefit of switching architectures.

suggestions.title: Side-Channel Resistance Profiling
suggestions.desc: Conduct a formal security comparison focusing on side-channel leakage (like L1 Terminal Fault) between MicroVMs and traditional VMs. Determine if the reduced attack surface of a MicroVM compensates for the potentially less mature isolation boundaries compared to KVM.

suggestions.title: MicroVM-Based Edge Function Hosting
suggestions.desc: Implement a lightweight hosting platform specifically for Raspberry Pi or ARM edge devices using MicroVMs. Show how MicroVMs can enable multi-tenant 'Function-as-a-Service' at the edge where memory and CPU resources are extremely constrained.

title: Evaluating the Ability of LLMs to Interpret, Optimize and Translate LLVM IR
authors: Mueller, Reuven
abstract: This study investigates whether modern state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) can interpret, optimize, and translate low-level intermediate representations (IR) used in compilers and binary translation software. We evaluate LLM performance on LLVM IR across three tasks: 1) interpreting the underlying algorithmic behavior, 2) identifying missed optimization opportunities, and generating improved IR variants, 3) Translating IR between AArch64 and x86-64 targets. To ensure correctness, all LLM generated IR is checked using a validation pipeline that verifies its syntax, structural correctness, compiles it into machine code, and executes it on randomized test data. Early results show that LLMs can perform non-trivial IR transformations on top of existing LLVM-O3 optimizations, highlighting their potential role in future compiler and binary translation workflows.

suggestions.title: LLM-Powered JIT Optimization Feedback Loop
suggestions.desc: Integrate an LLM into the LLVM compilation pipeline where it acts as a 'super-optimizer.' The system should generate candidate IR variants, pass them through a formal verifier (like Alive2), and keep only the provably correct, faster versions.

suggestions.title: Smart-IR: VS Code Extension for Binary Analysis
suggestions.desc: Build an IDE extension that uses LLMs to provide natural language explanations of LLVM IR code. Market it to security researchers and reverse-engineers who need to understand the 'intent' of obfuscated or low-level binary code during audits.

suggestions.title: Fine-Tuning CodeLLama for Cross-Architecture Translation
suggestions.desc: Research the effectiveness of fine-tuning an open-source model specifically on AArch64 and x86-64 IR pairs. Compare its performance against general-purpose LLMs in maintaining functional equivalence and handling architecture-specific flags.

suggestions.title: Automated Porting Assistant for Legacy Binaries
suggestions.desc: Create a tool that takes IR from legacy x86 systems and suggests modern, optimized AArch64 equivalents for deployment on AWS Graviton processors. Target enterprises looking to modernize their cloud infrastructure while reducing compute costs.

title: TADPS: Thrashing-Aware Dynamic Priority Scheduler for Virtual Memory Systems
authors: Ahmed, Nasim
abstract: Modern operating systems use multiprogramming and virtual memory to run multiple programs efficiently. When memory demand is high, excessive page swapping causes thrashing, degrading performance. This work introduces a thrashing-aware dynamic priority scheduler (TADPS) that uses page fault frequency to guide CPU scheduling. Processes with high page faults get lower priority, allowing others to progress efficiently. Tests under different memory pressures show that this approach improves CPU use, throughput, and stability compared with baseline schedulers such as FCFS and RR. Specifically, TADPS maintained superior throughput and efficiency, and consistently lower average turnaround time than FCFS and RR.

suggestions.title: TADPS Integration with Compressed RAM (zSwap)
suggestions.desc: Modify the TADPS scheduler to coordinate with Linux's zSwap or zRam. Instead of just lowering priority, the scheduler could trigger proactive memory compression for high-page-fault processes before they hit disk-based thrashing.

suggestions.title: Gaming-Mode Process Prioritizer for Linux Desktop
suggestions.desc: Build a user-space daemon that uses TADPS logic to ensure background updates or browser tabs never cause thrashing that impacts a foreground game’s frame time. Provide a UI for users to 'lock' specific apps into high-memory-priority tiers.

suggestions.title: Predictive Thrashing using LSTM-RNN
suggestions.desc: Extend the scheduler's logic by replacing static page fault frequency thresholds with a lightweight LSTM that predicts imminent thrashing based on the *rate of change* in memory demand. Evaluate if this 'proactive' approach reduces CPU idle time better than TADPS.

suggestions.title: Container Resource-Pressure Management for Docker
suggestions.desc: Apply the TADPS logic to a container orchestrator. If a specific container starts thrashing, the system could automatically throttle its CPU share at the cgroup level to prevent 'noisy neighbor' effects from crashing other containers on the same host.

title: User-Level GPU Right-Sizing in HPC: A Framework for Predicting Training Runtime
authors: Zhang, Yinning; Chowdhoury, s mtanvir faysal alam
abstract: Graphics Processing Unit (GPU) resources in High-Performance Computing (HPC) systems are frequently underutilized due to inaccurate user-provided run time estimates. This research develops a machine learning framework for predicting neural network training time from architectural features, dataset size, and other hyperparameters. This approach can be implemented on any HPC systems without requiring hardware access or runtime profiling as other preceding methods do. We sampled neural network models from the NATS-Bench benchmark and used 3 benchmark datasets to generate 400 training configurations. We used these 400 data points to build regression models and found that the best model, Gradient Boosting Regressor, can achieve an R² of 0.961 with prediction errors averaging less than one minute, demonstrating the feasibility of this proposed framework.

suggestions.title: HPC Job-Scheduler Plugin for Slurm
suggestions.desc: Implement the ML framework as a plugin for the Slurm Workload Manager. The plugin should automatically intercept user job submissions, compare user-estimated time vs. ML-predicted time, and recommend 'right-sized' resource allocations to the cluster admin.

suggestions.title: Cloud-GPU Cost Optimization Dashboard
suggestions.desc: Develop a SaaS tool that helps researchers estimate the cost of training a model on various AWS/Azure GPU instances. Use the ML framework to predict runtime across different architectures (e.g., A100 vs. V100) and suggest the most cost-effective hardware.

suggestions.title: Architecture-Agnostic Feature Extraction
suggestions.desc: Research a method to predict runtime for *newly released* GPUs by using a 'hardware signature' approach. Instead of retraining on every new chip, the model should learn to interpolate performance based on TFLOPS, memory bandwidth, and cache hierarchy.

suggestions.title: Carbon Footprint Estimator for ML Training
suggestions.desc: Use the runtime predictions to calculate the estimated CO2 emission of a training job based on the GPU's TDP and the local power grid's carbon intensity. This could be marketed as a 'Green-AI' tool for corporate sustainability reporting.

title: Comparative Analysis of OS-level Security Vulnerabilities and Isolation Mechanisms in Hypervisors and Containers
authors: Das, Jiban Krisna
abstract: This project investigates the operating-system-level performance and isolation mechanism of Virtual Machines and Docker container. The experiment includes CPU/memory microbenchmarks, disk throughput tests, web-server latency measurements, multi-process scheduling stress and controlled security checks. We aim to quantify each benchmark under identical conditions. The study findings reveal that Docker consistently provides lower overhead and faster I/O due to its shared-kernel architecture, while VirtualBox maintains stronger isolation but introduces more scheduling and disk latency. The findings provide practical insights for the OS system designers to find better execution environments for security critical and performance-sensitive workloads.

suggestions.title: Hardened Rootless-Container Sandbox
suggestions.desc: Based on the security findings, develop a 'Best Practices' configuration template and a scanning tool that audits Docker setups. The tool should automatically apply Linux security modules (AppArmor/SELinux) to close the isolation gaps identified in your benchmarks.

suggestions.title: Compliance-First Orchestration Advisor
suggestions.desc: Build a decision-support tool for regulated industries (Healthcare/Finance). It should ingest the user's performance needs and compliance requirements (HIPAA/PCI) to recommend whether specific workloads belong in a MicroVM, a VM, or a Container.

suggestions.title: Side-Channel Vulnerability Benchmarking
suggestions.desc: Extend the security checks to include side-channel attacks like Spectre or Meltdown across the different isolation layers. Quantify how much the performance overhead increases when all relevant kernel mitigations are enabled in Docker vs. KVM.

suggestions.title: Multi-Tenant Edge Node Firewall
suggestions.desc: Create a networking layer for edge devices that uses the project's isolation data to dynamically adjust firewall rules. If a container is identified as 'high risk' due to shared-kernel vulnerabilities, the system could automatically migrate it to a VM-based sandbox.

title: Graph Attention Network based Downlink Channel Prediction using in Frequency Division Duplexed NextGen Networks
authors: Mhatre, Jui
abstract: In Frequency Division Duplex (FDD) 5G networks, downlink channel state information (CSI) must be estimated at the user equipment (UE) and fed back to the base station, a process that requires frequent CSI-RS transmission and uplink feedback, resulting in high overhead and energy consumption. This research proposes a novel base-station–centric framework that predicts the downlink channel matrix directly at the gNB, eliminating the need for continuous CSI-RS–based estimation at the UE. By leveraging uplink channel observations, geometric environment features, and learned mappings between uplink and downlink channel relationships, our model reconstructs the downlink MIMO channel with high fidelity. The system integrates ray-tracing-based dataset generated via NVIDIA Sionna, combined with graph attention networks and transformer encoders to capture spatial and temporal channel dependencies. We infer the downlink channel without per-slot CSI-RS transmission based in environment geometry and uplink transmission signals, significantly reducing signaling overhead while maintaining beamforming performance. This work enables a shift toward AI-assisted FDD systems where proactive channel prediction replaces periodic downlink probing, contributing to greener and more efficient 5G/6G networks.

suggestions.title: FPGA-Accelerated GAT Inference Engine
suggestions.desc: Design a hardware-accelerated version of the GAT framework using HLS (High-Level Synthesis) for FPGAs. This would demonstrate how the gNB can perform real-time downlink channel prediction within the strict millisecond-level constraints of 5G frames.

suggestions.title: Private-5G Spectrum Optimizer
suggestions.desc: Build a management console for private 5G networks (e.g., smart factories) that uses the GAT model to reduce overhead. Market it as a solution to increase the number of IoT devices a single base station can support by reclaiming CSI-RS bandwidth.

suggestions.title: Mobility-Aware GAT for High-Velocity UE
suggestions.desc: Research how the GAT model performs when the User Equipment (UE) is moving at high speeds (e.g., in a car or train). Incorporate temporal features into the graph attention mechanism to maintain prediction accuracy despite rapid channel changes.

suggestions.title: Federated Learning for CSI Privacy
suggestions.desc: Extend the framework to a federated learning setting where multiple base stations collaborate to improve the GAT model without sharing raw channel data. This addresses privacy concerns in multi-operator shared-infrastructure environments.

title: Decentralized Scheduling and Memory Management in a Simulated Multikernel OS Environment
authors: ISLAM, MD JAHIRUL
abstract: Multikernel operating systems treat each processor core as an independent computing node rather than shared memory. This experiments presents the design and implementation of a multikernel OS simulator that models both decentralized and global scheduling architectures across multiple simulated cores. Each core executes tasks using either Round Robin (RR) or Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling. The simulator incorporates memory management, task tracking, visualization, generating performance metrics including turnaround time, waiting time, CPU utilization, and heatmap. Experimental results demonstrate that scheduler architecture significantly influences system performance. Decentralized scheduling favors RR where in global scheduling SJF performs better through balanced workload distribution.

suggestions.title: Decentralized IPC Protocol Implementation
suggestions.desc: Add an Inter-Process Communication (IPC) layer to the multikernel simulator. Implement a message-passing interface that allows tasks on Core A to request data from Core B without using shared memory, and measure the resulting latency and throughput.

suggestions.title: NUMA-Aware Memory Allocation Policy
suggestions.desc: Research the impact of Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA) on the multikernel design. Implement a memory management policy that prioritizes local core memory and analyze how 'remote' memory access penalties affect the decentralized RR vs. SJF scheduling results.

suggestions.title: Interactive Multikernel Educational Lab
suggestions.desc: Transform the simulator into a web-based educational platform for OS courses. Add a 'Challenge Mode' where students must write their own scheduling logic in a sandboxed script to solve specific resource-contention scenarios and visualize the results.

suggestions.title: Simulating Edge-Fog Distributed Systems
suggestions.desc: Adapt the simulator to model a 'Fog Computing' environment where each 'core' is actually a separate edge device with varying capabilities. Test how the decentralized scheduling architecture handles device failures and network latency between the nodes.

title: Edge-LLM Anomaly Detection on Raspberry Pi: Syscall Dataset Collection and Prototype LLM Explanation Layer
authors: Shrestha, Shiva
abstract: This research work offers a light-weight, end-to-end, syscall-level anomaly detection approach for the Raspberry Pi platform. The proposal involves the collection of around 2000 NORMAL and 200 ANOMALY syscall observation groups using the Linux Auditd safe synthetic generators. The work also utilizes a prototype LLM Explanation Layer, allowing the provision of human-friendly explanations pertaining to identified anomalies leveraging small LLM models like the Gemma-3 1B, Phi-3 Mini, or other sub 1B LLMs employing the Ollama platform. The LLM inference layer in this research work has partial implementations, as the fine-tuning of the model remains to be done.

suggestions.title: Automated Seccomp Profile Generation
suggestions.desc: Build a tool that takes the LLM-explained anomalies and automatically generates or updates Docker/Kubernetes Seccomp profiles to block the specific malicious syscall sequences identified on the Raspberry Pi.

suggestions.title: Federated Syscall Learning with Flower
suggestions.desc: Implement a Federated Learning framework using the Flower (flwr.dev) library to train the anomaly detection model across a fleet of Raspberry Pis without ever sharing raw, sensitive syscall logs with a central server.

suggestions.title: LLM-Powered SOC Incident Responder
suggestions.desc: Develop a browser-based dashboard for Security Operation Center (SOC) analysts that pulls the local LLM explanations into a centralized view, using the Gemma-3 1B output to prioritize alerts based on severity and intent.

suggestions.title: Role-Specific Behavioral Profiling
suggestions.desc: Extend the dataset to include 'Role Profiles' (e.g., a Pi acting as a MQTT broker vs. a Pi acting as a camera) to fine-tune the LLM layer to understand that a syscall sequence normal for a web server is an anomaly for a sensor node.

title: Hybrid Virtualization Performance Modeling Using Monte Carlo Simulation
authors: Akhi, Amatul
abstract: This project presents a performance analysis of virtualization, containerization, and hybrid container-in-VM architectures in modern operating systems. Virtual machines provide strong isolation and system stability but incur higher CPU, memory, and startup costs. Containers offer lightweight and fast execution but rely on weaker isolation. To evaluate a balanced alternative, performance data was extracted from recent research and modeled using a Monte Carlo simulation with 1,000 randomized workloads. A unified Hybrid Efficiency Score (HES) was introduced to compare systems consistently, weighting efficiency at 70% and isolation at 30%. Simulation results demonstrate that hybrid systems achieve the highest efficiency–isolation balance, with an average HES of ~0.82, compared to containers (~0.78) and virtual machines (~0.62). Hybrid architectures significantly reduce CPU overhead to 14.86%, nearly one-third lower than VMs (29.91%), and provide better energy savings (31.84%) than both VMs (0%) and containers (24.94%). Startup time also remains moderate (3.01 s), bridging the gap between fast containers (1.04 s) and slow VMs (30.19 s). Overall, these findings highlight hybrid container-in-VM architectures as a promising approach for achieving better trade-offs in efficiency, isolation, and scalability across cloud, edge, and high-performance computing environments.

suggestions.title: Dynamic Container-in-VM Live Migrator
suggestions.desc: Create a Python-based controller using the libvirt API that uses the Monte Carlo model to predict when a hybrid container-in-VM workload will exceed resource thresholds and triggers a live migration to a bare-metal host.

suggestions.title: Firecracker MicroVM Performance Benchmarking
suggestions.desc: Apply the project's performance modeling to AWS Firecracker microVMs, comparing their 'cold start' and CPU overhead against traditional KVM and Docker to find the mathematical 'sweet spot' for serverless architectures.

suggestions.title: Cloud Infrastructure Cost-Efficiency Calculator
suggestions.desc: Build a web tool that inputs user workload types and utilizes the Monte Carlo simulation data to recommend the most cost-effective AWS/Azure instance type (e.g., Fargate vs. EC2 vs. Nitro Enclaves).

suggestions.title: Edge-K3s Hybrid Overhead Analysis
suggestions.desc: Port the performance study to ARM architectures like Jetson Nano or Raspberry Pi to model the specific overhead of running K3s (lightweight Kubernetes) inside virtualized environments versus native execution.

title: Market-Driven Endurance Credits for Page Retention (MECR) in Hybrid DRAM–NVM Systems
authors: Ridwan, A E M
abstract: This work proposes MECR, a lightweight market-driven page-retention model for hybrid DRAM–NVM memory systems. MECR integrates a contextual bandit learner with endurance-aware credit bidding to optimize hit rate, fairness, and NVM lifetime. A deep verification layer ensures safe eviction decisions under wear-sensitive workloads. Experimental evaluation using synthetic memory traces shows stable accuracy, improved fairness, and adaptive DRAM allocation.

suggestions.title: Linux Kernel Module for MECR Integration
suggestions.desc: Implement the MECR credit-bidding logic as a Linux kernel module within the Memory Management (MM) subsystem to manage actual physical page migrations between DRAM and NVM (like Intel Optane).

suggestions.title: Deep Q-Network for Tiered CXL Memory
suggestions.desc: Replace the contextual bandit learner with a Deep Q-Network (DQN) designed for three-tier systems, managing page retention across HBM (High Bandwidth Memory), standard DRAM, and CXL-attached remote NVM.

suggestions.title: Database Buffer Pool Wear-Leveler
suggestions.desc: Integrate the MECR model into the buffer pool management of RocksDB or PostgreSQL to prolong the life of underlying NVMe storage by prioritizing low-wear pages for high-frequency write operations.

suggestions.title: Energy-Efficient AI Model Weight Paging
suggestions.desc: Apply the market-driven credit system to manage the paging of Large Language Model (LLM) weights between GPU VRAM and System NVM, maximizing inference speed while minimizing energy-intensive data movements.

title: Environmental Protection: Development of a Real-Time Multi-Stream Water Quality Monitoring System
authors: Muritala, Faruk
abstract: Water quality monitoring is crucial for environmental protection, public health, and ecosystem sustainability. With increasing pressures from urbanization, agricultural runoff, and climate change, robust data-driven approaches are essential for early detection of water quality degradation and informed decision-making in environmental conservation efforts. Current water quality monitoring relies on reactive threshold exceedances, failing to detect gradual degradation and multi-parameter deterioration patterns. This creates delayed response to pollution events and missed opportunities for preventive intervention in one of Queensland's most vital water systems. The importance objective is to implement and evaluate a Real-Time Multi-Stream Monitoring system for early detection of water quality deterioration by monitoring key parameters simultaneously, using the Brisbane River data for baseline establishment and testing detection performance across multiple control limit configurations.

suggestions.title: LoRaWAN Mesh Water Sensor Network
suggestions.desc: Build a hardware prototype using ESP32 microcontrollers and LoRa modules to transmit multi-stream sensor data (pH, Turbidity, DO) over long distances from remote river sites to a central monitoring gateway.

suggestions.title: Adversarial GANs for Contamination Simulation
suggestions.desc: Use Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to create synthetic 'chemical spill' datasets, allowing the multi-stream monitoring system to be trained on rare, catastrophic events that lack historical real-world data.

suggestions.title: Public Health Early Warning SaaS
suggestions.desc: Develop a React-based interactive map that visualizes real-time degradation and uses the Twilio API to send automated SMS warnings to local water treatment facilities and downstream communities when thresholds are projected to be hit.

suggestions.title: Smart Hydroponics Nutrient Optimizer
suggestions.desc: Adapt the multi-parameter monitoring system for vertical indoor farms, using the real-time data to automate the dosing of nutrients and pH balancers through a Raspberry Pi-controlled pump system.

title: Evaluating Generalization and Adaptation of Learning-Based Schedulers for Directed Acyclic Graph Workloads
authors: Rabia, Rabia
abstract: Learning-based schedulers such as Decima can optimize directed acyclic graph (DAG) workloads, yet their robustness under changing workload conditions is not well understood. This project evaluates how a Decima-trained policy transfers across different workload scenarios using an automated training and testing pipeline. Results show that the scheduler generalizes well to a workload with the same job scale, achieving a 1.9% improvement in average job completion time. Performance remains stable under a larger workload, but a shift in arrival pattern leads to an 83.7% increase in completion time and reduced fairness. These findings highlight both the potential and the limitations of learned scheduling policies, emphasizing the need for adaptive methods such as fine-tuning for reliable use in dynamic cluster environments.

suggestions.title: Kubernetes Custom DAG Scheduler
suggestions.desc: Implement the Decima-trained policy as a custom Kubernetes scheduler using the 'client-go' library to optimize the placement of complex Spark or Airflow DAG tasks across a distributed cluster.

suggestions.title: Heterogeneous Cluster-Aware Scheduling
suggestions.desc: Extend the Decima policy to account for a mix of CPU and GPU nodes, modeling the data transfer latency between hardware types as a variable in the DAG optimization process.

suggestions.title: Serverless Workflow Cost Optimizer
suggestions.desc: Apply the learning-based scheduler to AWS Step Functions or Google Cloud Workflows to minimize execution costs by strategically grouping DAG nodes to reduce data egress and invocation overhead.

suggestions.title: CI/CD Pipeline Execution Shuffler
suggestions.desc: Use the generalization findings to build a GitHub Actions optimizer that reorders job dependencies in complex build pipelines to maximize parallel execution and minimize total CI/CD wall-clock time.

title: Optimizing CPU Scheduling for Deep Learning and LLM Inference Using ONNX Runtime
authors: KHAN, MOHAMMOD AKIB
abstract: Modern applications rely on AI models that must perform real-time predictions on resource-constrained edge devices like laptops. The default OS scheduler often increases context switching, which slows down deep learning and LLM inference. Since these models depend heavily on parallel processing, efficient CPU scheduling becomes essential. In this project, we analyzed how core pinning and thread-level parallelism improve inference performance on a Windows system. Using multiple micro-batch sizes. We compare latency, throughput, and per-sample inference time. The goal is to show how simple OS-level optimization can significantly improve real-time performance for both deep learning models and LLM.

suggestions.title: Thermal-Aware Dynamic Affinity Governor
suggestions.desc: Develop a C++ background service for Windows that monitors CPU temperature and automatically re-pins ONNX threads to different cores to prevent thermal throttling during heavy LLM inference sessions.

suggestions.title: NUMA-Aware Large Model Parallelism
suggestions.desc: Extend the core-pinning research to multi-socket server environments, implementing NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) aware thread allocation to avoid the latency of cross-socket memory fetches for 70B+ parameter models.

suggestions.title: Real-Time Audio DSP Plugin with ONNX
suggestions.desc: Create a VST3 audio plugin that uses the optimized ONNX runtime to run real-time AI noise suppression or style transfer, ensuring the processing remains within the strict 5-10ms buffer window of modern DAWs.

suggestions.title: Android NDK Core-Affinity Optimizer
suggestions.desc: Port the Windows core-pinning logic to Android using the NDK and ONNX Runtime Mobile, targeting 'Big.LITTLE' ARM architectures to force inference onto high-performance cores for better mobile gaming AI.

title: Energy-Aware Operating Systems for Edge Artificial Intelligence Inference
authors: Jahan, Nursat
abstract: Edge Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to running AI inference directly on local devices such as wearables, sensors, and mobile systems rather than relying on cloud computing. The growth of Edge AI has created strong demand for efficient inference on resource-limited devices. Edge AI devices must perform real-time inference while operating under strict battery constraints. Although significant model optimizations exist for managing power-intensive inference models, operating system (OS) level support is limited. Existing OS schedulers often neglect energy limits in edge devices as they prioritize fairness or throughput. In this research we proposed an OS level framework to bridge this gap by introducing a Dynamic Energy-Aware Scheduler (DEAS) that adjusts CPU frequency and number of active cores based on workload conditions to reduce energy per inference. The preliminary simulation results show improved performance per watt, confirming that dynamic scheduling at the OS-level can significantly reduce energy consumption in AI edge systems.

suggestions.title: DVFS-Synced Inference Engine
suggestions.desc: Modify the Linux 'cpufreq' governor to synchronize Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling with the layers of a CNN, dropping frequency during less compute-intensive layers to save battery on edge devices.

suggestions.title: Precision-Scaling OS Scheduler
suggestions.desc: Research an OS-level mechanism that forces AI tasks to switch from Float32 to INT4 precision dynamically when the battery drops below 15%, trading off model accuracy for system longevity.

suggestions.title: Solar-Powered AI Surveillance Gateway
suggestions.desc: Build a solar-powered hardware kit using a Raspberry Pi Zero and a LiPo management board that uses this energy-aware OS to intelligently schedule person-detection tasks only during high-charge periods.

suggestions.title: Wearable Fall-Detection Lifetime Extender
suggestions.desc: Optimize the scheduling of an ESP32 or Nordic nRF52 chip running a TinyML fall-detection model, aiming to achieve a 6-month battery life by managing sleep states between sensor polls.

similar: legacy:UR-031|Spring 2021|an empirical study of thermal attacks on edge platforms

title: Can Mental Health Apps Really Help Caregivers? Usability Findings from Human-in-the-Loop NLP and Sentiment-Aware Analytics
authors: Salma, Syeda Umme
abstract: Caregivers face distinctive emotional and logistical burdens, yet many mental-health apps overlook their needs and show usability issues. We introduce an automated pipeline that analyzes 317K app-store reviews from 9 apps, mapping them to Nielsen’s usability components and heuristics, together with sentiment. To assess reliability, we run a human–AI agreement study (N=50) where a domain expert (A2) and a non- expert (A1) label reviews. For heuristics, the pipeline achieves 66% exact agreement and moderate ?=0.579 with the expert, outperforming human–human agreement; components remain harder, revealing a need to refine the codebook (e.g., learnability vs satisfaction). Complementary clustering and sentiment analyses highlight recurrent issues such as interface overload and inaccessible onboarding, positioning our pipeline as a pre-annotation aid for evidence-driven app evaluation.

suggestions.title: Automated Usability Bug-Tracker for GitHub
suggestions.desc: Create a tool that scrapes new Google Play/App Store reviews, processes them through the sentiment-heuristic pipeline, and automatically opens formatted Jira or GitHub issues for developers.

suggestions.title: Multimodal Caregiver Sentiment Analysis
suggestions.desc: Expand the project to include voice-memo data from caregivers, using OpenAI Whisper for transcription and comparing the sentiment expressed in speech versus written text reviews.

suggestions.title: Caregiver-Centric App Certification Benchmarking
suggestions.desc: Launch a B2B benchmarking service that provides mental health app developers with a 'Caregiver Usability Score' based on the project’s heuristics, offering actionable design recommendations.

suggestions.title: Empathy-Tuned LLM Chatbot Refinement
suggestions.desc: Use the identified usability gaps and negative sentiment patterns to fine-tune a Llama-3 model to act as a more empathetic virtual assistant specifically for caregivers, avoiding common 'clinical' pitfalls.

title: Continuous Monitoring of Cardiovascular Risk From Smartwatch Data Using a Knowledge Distillation Framework
authors: Jahan, Nursat
abstract: Cardiovascular Disease (CVD) is one of the leading causes of global health concern, but current risk assessments are limited to episodic clinical visits. Most machine learning (ML) models trained on clinical data offer high accuracy but are not practical for continuous monitoring. Smartwatch-based wearables provide continuous real-time physiological data but lack clinical validation for robust risk prediction outside the clinical setting. To bridge this gap, we proposed a novel teacher-student knowledge distillation framework that transfers knowledge of complex and large EHR datasets to a small Fitbit smartwatch dataset-based prediction model. The student model achieves promising accuracy, identifying all types of derived CVD risk profile groups. Feature importance analysis revealed that daily average steps and sedentary time are the most prominent wearable-derived CVD risk predictors. Our study introduces a non-invasive continuous health monitoring framework, demonstrating that passively collected daily metrics can be transformed to clinically powerful early warnings of an individual’s long-term cardiovascular health plan.

suggestions.title: On-Device WearOS Student Deployment
suggestions.desc: Convert the distilled student model into a TensorFlow Lite Micro format and deploy it to a WearOS or Apple Watch device to provide real-time CVD risk alerts without needing an active phone connection.

suggestions.title: Cross-Manufacturer Sensor Robustness Study
suggestions.desc: Evaluate the teacher-student framework across data from different sensors (Apple vs. Fitbit vs. Garmin) to determine if the student model can effectively filter out manufacturer-specific hardware noise.

suggestions.title: FHIR-Compliant Telehealth Integration API
suggestions.desc: Build a backend API using the HL7 FHIR standard that allows the continuous CVD risk scores from the student model to be automatically synced with a patient's clinical Electronic Health Record (EHR).

suggestions.title: Lifestyle-Correlation Heart Health Tracker
suggestions.desc: Develop a mobile app that correlates the CVD risk model output with user-logged data like stress levels, sleep duration, and caffeine intake to visualize the immediate impact of lifestyle choices on cardiovascular risk.

title: Zero-Day Host-Based Intrusion Detection via Hybrid Deep Sequence Modeling of SystemCalls
authors: Salma, Syeda Umme
abstract: Protecting endpoints has become increasingly challenging, as adversaries have been effective in bypassing defenses. Traditional signature-based Host-based IDS performs quite well at recognizing known patterns but often struggles with previously unseen activity. This study incorporates deep neural sequence modeling with classic OS telemetry to flag novel behavior from Linux system-cell traces. This study design is paired with a sequence encoder over syscall streams, incorporating lightweight statistical signals that are derived from process activity. We believe that our hybrid neural network approach will outperform conventional baselines and boost recall on unknown attacks while maintaining low false-positive rates, providing a practical and reproducible path to stronger host intrusion detection.

suggestions.title: eBPF-Powered Real-Time Detector
suggestions.desc: Rewrite the syscall collection layer using eBPF (Extended Berkeley Packet Filter) to feed the sequence encoder directly in the kernel, enabling sub-millisecond detection of zero-day exploits.

suggestions.title: Adversarial Syscall Injection Testing
suggestions.desc: Develop a framework to test the HIDS against 'mimicry attacks' where malicious syscalls are camouflaged within normal sequences, then use those results to perform adversarial training on the sequence encoder.

suggestions.title: Kubernetes Sidecar Threat Shield
suggestions.desc: Package the HIDS as a lightweight sidecar container for Kubernetes pods that monitors the host's syscall stream specifically for signs of container escape or lateral movement between microservices.

suggestions.title: Exploit Forensic Reconstruction Tool
suggestions.desc: Build a post-incident tool that uses the sequence encoder to 'replay' captured syscall logs, visually highlighting the exact timestamp and syscall sequence where the zero-day attack deviated from the baseline.

similar: legacy:GR-044|Spring 2021|an efficient intrusion detection framework based on federated learning for iot networks.

title: AutoTrader-AgentEdge
authors: Regan, Christopher
abstract: This work presents AutoTrader-AgentEdge, a human-in-loop trading system that positions AI agents as collaborative partners rather than autonomous replacements. We demonstrate that multi-indicator consensus voting combined with human approval achieves superior risk-adjusted returns while maintaining interpretability and control. Core Contribution: A production-ready VoterAgent implementing democratic voting between MACD momentum and RSI extremes, generating transparent trading signals for human evaluation. Unlike black-box automation, our interactive CLI augments trader expertise through interpretable consensus logic. The human retains final decision authority at all critical junctures. Validated Performance: Empirical validation demonstrates multi-indicator voting superiority over single-indicator automation: Sharpe ratio 0.856 vs 0.841, max drawdown -10.10% vs -10.58%, win rate 51.4% vs 31.9%. Extended testing shows 11.2% better relative performance in volatile markets, validating risk management focus. Implementation: Built on the Microsoft AutoGen framework with an extensible multi-agent architecture. SQLite caching achieves 8-10x performance improvement. Interactive CLI enables natural language trade discussion with human approval gates, ensuring trader control while reducing cognitive load. Alpaca broker integration for paper trading. Key Insight: Transparent, interpretable methods build trust and enable effective human-AI collaboration. By prioritizing augmentation over automation, we demonstrate that AI serves traders best as a decision support tool that preserves human judgment while systematically reducing errors. Implications: Contributes to augmented trading research - systems designed to enhance rather than replace human expertise. This work validates that human-in-loop architectures can achieve both superior risk-adjusted returns AND maintained trader control, challenging the "automation-at-all-costs" paradigm prevalent in algorithmic trading.

suggestions.title: Sentiment-Weighted Consensus Voting
suggestions.desc: Integrate a 'SentimentAgent' into the voter pool using the NewsAPI and VADER sentiment analysis. This agent would analyze financial headlines and social media trends to provide a 'market mood' vote that can override technical indicators like RSI during high-volatility news events.

suggestions.title: Dynamic Weighting via Reinforcement Learning
suggestions.desc: Replace the static democratic voting system with a Deep Q-Network (DQN) that learns to weight the MACD and RSI agents based on the current market regime (e.g., trending vs. ranging). Train the model on historical S&P 500 data to identify which 'voter' is most reliable in specific volatility clusters.

suggestions.title: Mobile Approval Node with Real-Time Backtesting
suggestions.desc: Build a React Native mobile application that serves as the 'Human-in-loop' interface, receiving push notifications for trade signals. Include a 'mini-backtest' feature that shows the human user how that specific signal would have performed over the last 30 days before they click 'Approve'.

suggestions.title: Cross-Asset Correlation Filter
suggestions.desc: Expand AgentEdge to manage a multi-asset portfolio by adding a 'CorrelationAgent' using the Alpha Vantage API. This agent would vote against signals that would over-leverage the portfolio into highly correlated assets (e.g., blocking a BTC buy signal if the portfolio is already heavy in tech stocks).

title: Detecting Dealer Gamma Hedging Mechanics: How LLMs Identify Market Structure Without Context
authors: Regan, Christopher
abstract: We introduce obfuscation testing, a novel methodology for validating whether large language models detect structural market patterns through causal reasoning rather than temporal association. Testing three dealer hedging constraint patterns (gamma positioning, stock pinning, 0DTE hedging) on 242 trading days (95.6% coverage) of S&P 500 options data, we find LLMs achieve 71.5% detection rate using unbiased prompts that provide only raw gamma exposure values without regime labels or temporal context. The WHO?WHOM?WHAT causal framework forces models to identify the economic actors (dealers), affected parties (directional traders), and structural mechanisms (forced hedging) underlying observed market dynamics. Critically, detection accuracy (91.2%) remains stable even as economic profitability varies quarterly, demonstrating that models identify structural constraints rather than profitable patterns. When prompted with regime labels, detection increases to 100%, but the 71.5% unbiased rate validates genuine pattern recognition. Our findings suggest LLMs possess emergent capabilities for detecting complex financial mechanisms through pure structural reasoning, with implications for systematic strategy development, risk management, and our understanding of how transformer architectures process financial market dynamics.

suggestions.title: Cross-Market Generalizability Audit
suggestions.desc: Apply the obfuscation testing methodology to Crude Oil (WTI) and Gold (GC) options data. This would determine if LLMs recognize universal hedging mechanics across different asset classes or if their 71.5% success rate is specific to the high liquidity and unique 'stock pinning' behavior of the S&P 500.

suggestions.title: LoRA Fine-Tuning for Market Structure Latency
suggestions.desc: Use Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune a Llama-3-8B model specifically on the 242-day dataset. Contrast the detection rate of the fine-tuned model against the base model to see if LLMs can 'learn' structural market reasoning beyond their initial training cutoff.

suggestions.title: Zero-Knowledge Institutional Sentiment Tracker
suggestions.desc: Develop a dashboard that uses the project's obfuscated prompt methodology to analyze institutional flows without exposing sensitive position labels. This tool would provide a 'Gamma Stress Score' to traders based purely on raw exposure values, maintaining privacy for the data provider.

suggestions.title: 0DTE Volatility Regime Classifier
suggestions.desc: Build a real-time monitoring tool that focuses specifically on the 0DTE (Zero Days to Expiration) data subset. Use the LLM to classify whether the current market hour is in a 'Gamma Neutral' or 'Short Squeeze' regime, providing actionable alerts for intraday scalpers.

title: A Safe Vision-Guided Robotic Injection Control Framework Based on Reinforcement Learning
authors: liu, Zhiguo
abstract: This project presents a modular vision-guided robotic framework for safe deltoid intramuscular injection. An external YOLO-based detector localizes the deltoid region and outputs a 3D injection point in the robot base frame. In NVIDIA Isaac Sim, a simulated myCobot 280 arm receives this point, and a deep reinforcement learning policy generates a safe approach pose under kinematic and safety constraints, while a deterministic controller executes the final straight-line insertion and withdrawal. A safety supervisor monitors target validity, joint limits and distance to a simplified arm model, triggering immediate stop and retraction when unsafe conditions arise.

suggestions.title: Sim-to-Real Domain Randomization Suite
suggestions.desc: Improve the RL policy's robustness by implementing domain randomization in NVIDIA Isaac Sim, varying skin tones, lighting conditions, and arm textures. This would prepare the model for physical deployment on a real myCobot 280 by ensuring the YOLO detector doesn't fail due to visual noise.

suggestions.title: Haptic-Feedback Integration for Bone Avoidance
suggestions.desc: Incorporate a force-torque sensor (simulated or real) into the RL state space. This allows the framework to detect 'hard resistance' (simulating bone contact) and triggers an immediate safe withdrawal protocol, moving beyond simple vision-guided positioning to tactile safety.

suggestions.title: Multi-Site Pediatric Injection Adaptation
suggestions.desc: Extend the YOLO-based detector and RL policy to identify and inject the vastus lateralis (thigh) region, which is the standard site for pediatric patients. This requires retraining the localization model on a dataset of infant/child anatomical models to account for scale and proportion differences.

suggestions.title: AR-Assisted Human Supervision Interface
suggestions.desc: Develop an Augmented Reality overlay for the Microsoft HoloLens that visualizes the robot's intended 'safe approach pose' and 'straight-line path' directly onto the patient's arm. This allows a human nurse to provide a final visual safety check before the RL policy executes the insertion.

title: Adaptive SCHED_DEADLINE on Linux for Robotic-Arm Control
authors: liu, Zhiguo
abstract: Industrial robot arms often run 500–1000 Hz control loops on general-purpose Linux. Most cycles are on time, but rare long scheduling delays can cause visible end-effector jitter, force spikes, and unstable insertion behavior. Static tuning of priorities and budgets cannot fully remove these long-tail outliers without wasting CPU. This work asks whether a lightweight adaptive layer on top of SCHED_DEADLINE can reduce high-percentile latency while keeping good utilization.

suggestions.title: eBPF Latency Mitigation Layer
suggestions.desc: Develop an eBPF (Extended Berkeley Packet Filter) program that monitors SCHED_DEADLINE runtime misses directly in the kernel. The program could dynamically adjust the 'runtime' and 'deadline' parameters in real-time to prevent end-effector jitter without the overhead of context switching to a userspace control layer.

suggestions.title: Comparative Analysis with PREEMPT_RT
suggestions.desc: Conduct a side-by-side performance benchmark between your adaptive layer on SCHED_DEADLINE and a standard Linux kernel patched with PREEMPT_RT. Use a high-speed camera or laser tracker to measure physical end-effector jitter and determine which kernel approach minimizes force spikes during high-speed insertions.

suggestions.title: Jitter-Aware ROS 2 Executor
suggestions.desc: Implement the adaptive scheduling logic as a custom ROS 2 (Robot Operating System) Executor.
